summary: World Cup debutants who earned real respect — under Bubista they played expansive football to qualify ahead of a strong Cameroon, losing just one of 10 games. An exciting cast of quick attackers; likely forced deep and onto the counter against Spain and Uruguay.
style: Best when they can seize the initiative against weaker sides — full-backs drift inside while talented No 10s combine with tricky wingers to pull compact defences apart. Against stronger opposition, expect a deep 4-4-2 with wingers tucking in to compact the middle, then breaking fast through the pace of Cabral and a streaking Livramento.

counterReliance.note: Expansive vs lesser sides; deep block and vertical counter vs the favourites

strengths: Quick, dynamic attackers who break at pace on the counter
Clever final-third rotations (full-backs inside, No 10s and wingers combining)
Defensive tenacity to win the ball and go (Livramento's winner vs Cameroon)
weaknesses: Likely pinned in their own third for long spells against Spain and Uruguay
Step up in level from CAF qualifying for a debutant

keyPlayers.name: Dailon Livramento
keyPlayers.note: Qualifying top scorer — streaks in behind on the counter; nicked the decisive goal vs Cameroon after barging Baleba off the ball

keyPlayers.name: Jamiro Monteiro
keyPlayers.note: A box of tricks with searing pace; drifts inside to make things happen

keyPlayers.name: Ryan Mendes
keyPlayers.note: Pace on the opposite flank to Monteiro

tactics: Counter with pace when pushed back, but back themselves to be expansive and rotate cleverly when they can take the initiative. They topped a group containing Cameroon — treat them as a quality side capable of surprising the favourites.
notes: First-ever World Cup; qualified ahead of Cameroon, beaten just once in 10.
sources: The Athletic — 2026 World Cup tactical guide, Group H (5 Jun 2026)
John Walker (@johnwalker_1986) — pre-tournament tactical scouting, May–Jun 2026
